name: Chemical Bonding
subject: chemistry

sections.title: I. What Are Chemical Bonds?
paragraphs: A chemical bond is a lasting attraction between atoms that enables the formation of molecules and compounds. Atoms bond because they are more stable when connected to other atoms than when they exist alone.

The key to understanding bonding is valence electrons — the electrons in the outermost shell of an atom. These are the electrons that participate in bonding. Atoms with full outer shells (like noble gases) are extremely stable and rarely bond. Every other atom is trying to achieve that same stability by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons.

There are three main types of chemical bonds: ionic bonds (electron transfer), covalent bonds (electron sharing), and metallic bonds (electron pooling). Each type results in different properties — ionic compounds form crystals and dissolve in water, covalent compounds form molecules with specific shapes, and metals conduct electricity.

sections.title: II. Ionic Bonds
paragraphs: An ionic bond forms when one atom transfers one or more electrons to another atom. The atom that loses electrons becomes a positively charged ion (cation), and the atom that gains electrons becomes a negatively charged ion (anion). The opposite charges attract, holding the ions together.

Ionic bonds typically form between metals (which easily lose electrons) and nonmetals (which easily gain electrons). For example, sodium (Na) has one valence electron it wants to lose, and chlorine (Cl) has seven valence electrons and wants one more. Sodium gives its electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻. The electrostatic attraction between these ions creates sodium chloride — table salt.

Ionic compounds have high melting points because the electrostatic forces between ions are very strong. They form crystal lattices — regular, repeating 3D arrangements of alternating positive and negative ions. They conduct electricity when dissolved in water because the ions are free to move.

sections.title: III. Covalent Bonds
paragraphs: A covalent bond forms when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Unlike ionic bonds where electrons are transferred, covalent bonds involve mutual sharing. Both atoms contribute electrons to a shared pair that orbits both nuclei.

Covalent bonds typically form between two nonmetals. For example, two hydrogen atoms each contribute one electron to form H₂. The shared pair of electrons holds both atoms together. This is a single covalent bond.

Atoms can share more than one pair of electrons. A double bond shares two pairs (like O₂), and a triple bond shares three pairs (like N₂). Triple bonds are shorter and stronger than double bonds, which are shorter and stronger than single bonds.

The shape of covalent molecules is determined by VSEPR theory — electron pairs around a central atom repel each other and arrange themselves as far apart as possible. This is why water (H₂O) is bent, carbon dioxide (CO₂) is linear, and methane (CH₄) is tetrahedral.

curiosityBomb: The bond between nitrogen atoms in N₂ is one of the strongest bonds in nature — it takes 945 kJ/mol to break it. That's why nitrogen gas is so unreactive, and why industrial nitrogen fixation (the Haber process) requires temperatures of 450°C and pressures of 200 atmospheres.
